metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete condition that triggers the usage-plausibility warning (e.g., 'warn when both prompt_tokens and completion_tokens are 0 after a call that returned content') so the AC is unambiguously testable.
findings.finding: The plan never defines the threshold for 'usage looks zeroed/implausible', leaving the executor to invent a definition. This is a defaultable gap that should be stated explicitly (e.g., 'warn when both prompt_tokens and completion_tokens are 0 after a non-trivial call').
findings.location: What section: 'A usage-plausibility telemetry warning (never a block) when per-provider usage looks zeroed/implausible' and AC3: 'Usage-plausibility emits a telemetry warning and never blocks a gate.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Triggers when `usage.request_tokens == 0 and usage.response_tokens == 0` on a response with non-empty output; verified by a unit test that patches the provider adapter to return zeroed usage and asserts a WARNING-level log entry is emitted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (e.g., using `unittest.mock.patch` + call-count assertion) that invokes the construction-time check for the same model twice and asserts the underlying introspection runs exactly once, making the cache guarantee testable.
findings.finding: AC2 claims the construction-time capability check is 'cached, invoked once per model, not per call', but no described step or test proves the once-per-model property — `make test` alone does not verify invocation count.
findings.location: AC: 'A construction-time capability check fails fast (cached, invoked once per model, not per call).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test plan: 'A unit test patches the internal capability-probe function, constructs the same model twice, and asserts the probe was called exactly once (functools.lru_cache or equivalent). Verified via `make test`.' Also clarify the cache key (model class? model name string?) in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the cache mechanism for the construction-time capability check (e.g., 'module-level `@lru_cache` on the resolved model-string prefix') so the 'once per model, not per call' invariant is implementable without ambiguity.
findings.finding: The 'cached, invoked once per model, not per call' specification for the construction-time capability check does not define the cache mechanism or its scope, leaving the implementation free to use a per-instance check (equivalent to per-call if instances are per-call) rather than a module-level LRU keyed on model string — which would silently violate the stated 'not per call' invariant.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria (AC line 2: 'A construction-time capability check fails fast (cached, invoked once per model, not per call)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Cache via a module-level `@functools.lru_cache` keyed on the resolved model-string prefix (e.g., `anthropic`, `openai`, `google-gla`), called from `PydanticAIRunner.__init__` or `preflight()`; the cache is process-scoped so repeated construction of a runner for the same model string pays the check once.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete trigger condition for the usage-plausibility warning (e.g., 'emit when execution_mode=agentic AND both input_tokens=0 AND output_tokens=0 after a non-empty run') to distinguish the #5360 silent-zero bug from legitimate zero-usage cases (single_turn, FunctionModel tests).
findings.finding: The usage-plausibility telemetry warning lacks a trigger condition, making the implementer's threshold choice arbitrary and likely to produce false positives (e.g., warning on every single_turn call where `requests=0` is a legitimate and expected value, not an indicator of a zeroed-usage provider bug).
findings.location: ## What / Acceptance Criteria (AC line 3: 'Usage-plausibility emits a telemetry warning and never blocks a gate')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and document the exact pydantic-ai 1.107.0 API used to introspect a constructed Agent's tool list (e.g. `agent._function_tools`, `agent.tool_names()`, or the AgentInfo object from a FunctionModel call) so the test can be grounded to a specific, version-stable surface.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'testable introspection' for 'tools actually offered to the agent' but does not identify the specific pydantic-ai API surface used to inspect which tools an Agent was constructed with. Agent._function_tools is private; no public `agent.tools` or equivalent is documented in pydantic-ai 1.107.0. Without naming the introspection mechanism the test cannot be written correctly.
findings.location: Verified library behavior section / AC line 1 ('Tests fail if the expected tools are not offered')
findings.suggested_fix: Use a FunctionModel callback that receives AgentInfo (which has .function_tools) as the introspection vehicle — the existing test_runner_pydantic_budget.py already uses this pattern (AgentInfo.function_tools[0].name at line 35). Document this as the chosen seam in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for the construction-time provider tool-calling check: name the pydantic-ai API, attribute, or structural property that determines whether a resolved model string supports tools (e.g. an allowlist of known tool-capable provider prefixes, or a pydantic-ai internal flag), and verify it offline before committing to the implementation.
findings.finding: The plan promises a construction-time provider tool-calling capability check ('the configured provider supports tool-calling for tool-using ops') but neither the plan text nor the experiment log identifies what pydantic-ai API surfaces this property. pydantic-ai 1.107.0 has no documented `Model.supports_tool_calling` or equivalent; detecting it typically requires either a trial call or class-name inspection — both of which have significant drawbacks.
findings.location: AC line 2 ('A construction-time capability check fails fast'); 'What' section bullet 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact expected tool names (e.g., 'read_file', 'list_directory', 'search_files', 'show_ticket') and the pydantic-ai introspection path (e.g., `agent._function_tools` attribute name in 1.107.0) that the tool-offering test queries, co-located with the AC.
findings.finding: The test asserting 'the expected tools are actually offered to the agent' never defines what 'expected tools' means: which tool names, for which RunRequest configuration, and which pydantic-ai introspection API to query — so an implementor cannot write this test without guessing.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria (AC line 1: 'Tests fail if the expected tools are not offered')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What: 'The tools-offered test constructs a PydanticAIRunner with a known RunRequest (agentic mode, allow_comment=False) and asserts that `{"read_file", "list_directory", "search_files", "show_ticket"} ⊆ {t.name for t in agent._function_tools}` using pydantic-ai 1.107.0\'s `Agent._function_tools` attribute.' Confirm the attribute name via the experiment harness.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the construction-time capability check's caching key (e.g., resolved model string), caching mechanism (e.g., `functools.lru_cache`), the pydantic-ai attribute queried to detect non-tool-calling providers, and the exception type raised on failure.
findings.finding: The construction-time capability check ('cached, invoked once per model, not per call') references a caching mechanism and a 'mis-provisioned provider' concept whose vocabulary (cache key, cache mechanism, what attribute to inspect, what exception to raise) is never defined, making the spec unimplementable as written.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria (AC line 2: 'A construction-time capability check fails fast (cached, invoked once per model, not per call)') / Files: src/rebar/llm/runner.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the plausibility threshold (e.g., 'warn when input_tokens == 0 AND output_tokens == 0 AND model_override is None AND execution_mode == agentic'), the log level/channel, and how the check exempts test/offline model paths.
findings.finding: The usage-plausibility telemetry warning never defines the threshold for 'zeroed/implausible', how to distinguish a genuinely zeroed provider adapter from the existing defensive empty-dict fallback in _extract_usage (which already silently returns {} for missing usage), or which logger/channel receives the warning.
findings.location: ## What ('A usage-plausibility telemetry warning (never a block) when per-provider usage looks zeroed/implausible') / ## Acceptance Criteria (AC line 3)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What: 'The usage-plausibility warning fires in _extract_usage or at the call site in runner.py when: model_override is None (real provider path), execution_mode is agentic, and both input_tokens and output_tokens are 0. It emits logger.warning(...) and never raises. It is suppressed on the model_override/test path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether pydantic-ai 1.107.0 exposes an offline API to query provider tool-calling support (e.g. `ModelInfo.supports_tools`, `AgentModel.info()`, or similar), and if not, explicitly state that the construction-time check is a static model-string prefix allowlist — both in the plan and in the implementation comment.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'a construction-time capability check that fails fast on a mis-provisioned provider for a tool-using op' but does not identify which pydantic-ai 1.107.0 API provides an OFFLINE query of whether a given provider/model supports tool-calling. The experiment log confirms tool_timeout and tools exist on Agent.__init__, but says nothing about a provider capability introspection API.
findings.location: ## What — 'A one-time capability assertion at model construction' / src/rebar/llm/runner.py (construction-time check)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a described test (in tests/unit/) that asserts the usage-plausibility path emits a warning (e.g., via captured logs or a mock telemetry sink) and does NOT raise an exception, so AC3 is verifiable.
findings.finding: AC3 ('Usage-plausibility emits a telemetry warning and never blocks a gate') describes an observable behavioral guarantee but no test deliverable is described anywhere in the plan to verify it — the What section's test bullet only covers tools-offered, usage_limits mis-placement, and provider tool-calling; the usage-plausibility warning is listed as a separate artifact (not a test subject). This leaves AC3 unverifiable by the described deliverables.
findings.location: AC3; What (bullet 3: 'A usage-plausibility telemetry warning (never a block)'); Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the What section's test bullet to include: 'the usage-plausibility path emits a warning and does not raise' — and add a corresponding test file/function in tests/unit/ that monkeypatches a zeroed-usage provider response and asserts a warning is logged (e.g., using pytest's caplog or a mock telemetry emitter).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific pydantic-ai 1.107.0 attribute or API used to introspect the Agent's registered tool list post-construction (e.g. `agent._toolsets`, `agent.tool_defs()`, or the run-result inspection path), and confirm it is stable across the `>=1,<2` range pinned in pyproject.toml.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'testable introspection' path for detecting tools-actually-offered (pydantic-ai #5177 hook-returns-None scenario), but the experiment log does NOT name the specific pydantic-ai 1.107.0 attribute or API used to read back the Agent's registered tool list post-construction. If the introspection relies on a private/internal attribute (e.g. `Agent._tools`, `Agent._toolsets`), a minor version bump could silently break the test without a CI failure on the application path itself.
findings.location: ## Verified library behavior (experiment log) / ## Acceptance Criteria: 'Tests fail if the expected tools are not offered'
findings.suggested_fix: In the experiment log or plan body, record the exact expression used (e.g. `Agent(model, tools=tools)._toolsets` or `list(agent.iter_tools())`) and verify it is a public/documented attribute. If only a private attribute is available, note it and add a version-sentinel assertion (e.g. `assert pydantic_ai.__version__.startswith('1.')`) next to the introspection call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the caching mechanism and scope for the construction-time capability check (e.g. `@functools.lru_cache` keyed on the resolved model string at module level, with a note about cache invalidation in tests), and confirm the strategy handles test isolation (monkeypatching or cache-reset fixture).
findings.finding: The plan specifies the construction-time check is 'cached, invoked once per model' but does not define the caching strategy or the cache key. The existing preflight() in PydanticAIRunner is uncached and call-site-invoked; the plan proposes a different lifecycle without saying how (module-level lru_cache keyed on model string? class variable? module singleton dict?).
findings.location: AC line 2 ('cached, invoked once per model, not per call'); 'Files' section (src/rebar/llm/runner.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Use `@functools.lru_cache(maxsize=None)` on a module-level `_check_provider_capability(resolved: str)` function; expose a `_check_provider_capability.cache_clear()` in tests. This mirrors how rebar.config.mcp_readonly uses reset_config_cache() for test isolation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the `usage_limits`-placement test is behavioral (invokes the agent path with limits and asserts they are honored/passed through) or structural; if structural, add a rationale explaining why a source-grep or API-signature check is faithful to the invariant, or add a behavioral assertion.
findings.finding: The plan's AC for 'usage_limits is mis-placed on the constructor' does not specify whether the test is behavioral (actually invokes rebar's agent with limits and observes enforcement) or a structural/source-grep check (scans rebar call-sites for the absence of usage_limits in Agent(...)). If implemented as a source-grep or AST scan of rebar's own code, it is the source-grep-masquerading-as-behavioral-test anti-pattern — it locks in the structural pattern without verifying that limits are actually honored at runtime.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'usage_limits is mis-placed on the constructor' assertion; tests/unit/ (new)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC sub-item: 'The usage_limits test invokes rebar's agent runner with a usage_limits value on run* and asserts the value reaches pydantic-ai's run_sync (e.g., via spy/mock on run_sync), not merely that Agent.__init__ lacks the parameter in the library API.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the assertion strategy for the usage_limits mis-placement test: define whether it tests that Agent() silently ignores the kwarg (behavioral: limits are not enforced when passed to constructor), or that the codebase pattern is absent (static), and which of these actually detects the #1987 failure mode.
findings.finding: The 'usage_limits mis-placed on the constructor' test invariant is never specified as an assertion strategy — the plan does not say whether the test checks that Agent() raises, that a static analysis rule fires, or that the codebase has no Agent(..., usage_limits=...) call.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria (AC line 1: 'usage_limits is mis-placed on the constructor')

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the cache key explicitly in the plan (e.g., 'keyed on the model identifier string, e.g. `"openai:gpt-4o"`, using `functools.lru_cache` on a module-level function') so the executor does not need to invent this design choice.
findings.finding: The caching key for the construction-time capability check is unspecified ('cached, invoked once per model') — 'per model' is ambiguous between per model class, per model name string, per model instance, or per (model, tool-set) tuple. An executor must invent the key, which affects correctness.
findings.location: What section: 'A one-time capability assertion at model construction (cached, not per-call) that fails fast on a mis-provisioned provider for a tool-using op.' and AC2.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Cache keyed on the model-identifier string (e.g. `provider:model-name`), implemented via `functools.lru_cache` on the module-level probe function in `runner.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit file target for the usage-plausibility telemetry warning (e.g., `src/rebar/llm/runner.py: _extract_usage()` or a new helper) to the Files section so the AC3 test knows what to assert against.
findings.finding: The 'usage-plausibility telemetry warning' (AC3, '#5360 cluster') has no named production file target — the Files section lists only `tests/unit/` and `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`, but the plan does not say which of these (or a different file) receives the warning-emission code.
findings.location: Files section (absent entry); AC: 'Usage-plausibility emits a telemetry warning and never blocks a gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the usage-plausibility threshold (e.g., as a named constant or config key) so it is visible and tunable without hunting through implementation code.
findings.finding: The usage-plausibility telemetry warning's threshold for 'zeroed/implausible' usage is nowhere defined or made configurable; if a numeric or heuristic threshold is hardcoded in the implementation, it will be difficult to tune as provider behaviors evolve.
findings.location: What section: 'A usage-plausibility telemetry warning (never a block) when per-provider usage looks zeroed/implausible.'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a named module-level constant (e.g., `USAGE_PLAUSIBILITY_MIN_TOKENS = 0`) or a config entry, and document in a comment which provider behaviors informed the choice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the construction-time capability check as an intentional behavioral change and add either an explicit rollback/back-out mechanism (e.g., a feature flag or opt-in toggle) or an expand-contract sequence (warn-only first, fail in a follow-up) so mis-provisioned deployments have a migration path.
findings.finding: The construction-time capability check introduces a new hard-failure mode (fast-fail at construction for mis-provisioned providers). For any existing deployment where a non-tool-calling provider is configured for a tool-using op, behavior changes from silent/deferred failure to immediate construction-time exception. The plan does not acknowledge this behavioral break, nor provide a rollback path, feature flag, or expand-contract sequence for affected deployments.
findings.location: What (bullet 2: 'A one-time capability assertion at model construction … that fails fast on a mis-provisioned provider'); Files: src/rebar/llm/runner.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under Scope or a new 'Migration / rollback' section: state explicitly that the check is a breaking change for mis-provisioned providers, and either (a) make the check warn-only initially (matching the telemetry-warning pattern) with a follow-up to harden it, or (b) gate it behind a config flag (e.g., REBAR_STRICT_CAPABILITY_CHECK=1) that can be disabled for a transition period.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the cache mechanism (e.g., `functools.lru_cache`, a thread-safe dict with a lock, or a class-level set), define who owns/creates it, ensure mutations are atomic (lock or CAS), and document a cache-invalidation/reset path for test isolation.
findings.finding: The 'cached, once per model' construction-time capability check introduces shared mutable state whose CREATE, UPDATE concurrency, and RETIRE lifecycle are unspecified — creating a risk of TOCTOU races in multi-threaded use and an inability to reset the cache in tests.
findings.location: ## What — 'A one-time capability assertion at model construction (cached, not per-call)' and AC line: 'A construction-time capability check fails fast (cached, invoked once per model, not per call)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `functools.lru_cache` on a module-level function (Python's lru_cache is thread-safe for read-after-write) keyed on a hashable model identifier, and expose a `cache_clear()` call (lru_cache provides this automatically) for test teardown. Document in the plan that tests must call `_check_provider_capability.cache_clear()` in setUp/teardown.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the PydanticAIRunner construction call sites (especially tests/interfaces/ and model_override paths) and confirm the construction-time capability check is a no-op or appropriately gated when model_override is set.
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate the external consumers of `PydanticAIRunner` that a construction-time capability check in `__init__` would affect — there are 14+ call sites across `src/rebar/llm/` subdirectories and `tests/interfaces/` that construct `PydanticAIRunner` directly or via `get_runner`, and a hard `__init__`-time assertion could break offline/test paths.
findings.location: Scope section; Files section: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether rebar's plain-function tool registration is at risk from #5177 (hook-None stripping); if not, reframe the 'tools actually offered' AC to name the actual threat it guards (e.g., execution_mode='single_turn' mis-routing, empty tools= list from a config branch failure) so the test is targeted correctly.
findings.finding: The plan names pydantic-ai bug #5177 (a hook returning None strips tools silently) as the motivating threat for 'tests that tools are actually offered', but rebar's current tool registration uses plain Python callables passed as `tools=` list items — not hook-decorated tools that have hook callbacks that can return None. If plain-function tools cannot trigger #5177, the proposed test guards against a failure mode that does not apply to rebar's construction pattern, making it a proxy test rather than a targeted invariant.
findings.location: ## What / Acceptance Criteria (AC line 1: 'Tests fail if the expected tools are not offered')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design note (ADR entry, CLAUDE.md section, or prominent module docstring in runner.py) explaining the construction-time capability check pattern, the pydantic-ai issues it guards (#5177, #6186, #1987, #5360), and why it is cached rather than per-call.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel construction-time capability-check pattern in runner.py with no accompanying ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design note, making the rationale and invariants invisible to future maintainers.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` (construction-time check); What/Scope sections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error handling (try/except with a clear logged warning or re-raise with context) in the construction-time capability check so that unexpected introspection errors produce an actionable message rather than an opaque crash.
findings.finding: The construction-time capability check has no stated error-handling for unexpected introspection failures (e.g., the pydantic-ai introspection API raising an exception due to a library upgrade), which could cause all tool-using agent construction to fail with an opaque error.
findings.location: What section: 'A one-time capability assertion at model construction (cached, not per-call) that fails fast on a mis-provisioned provider for a tool-using op.'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the introspection in a try/except catching at minimum AttributeError and TypeError; on unexpected error, log a warning with the pydantic-ai version and either degrade gracefully (skip check, emit warning) or re-raise with a message naming the library version and the expected attribute.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the title to reflect all three deliverables (tests, construction-time capability check, usage-plausibility telemetry warning), and add a note in Scope or AC acknowledging that existing runner.py callers constructing agents against non-tool-calling providers will now receive a fast-fail exception (and whether any callers need a guard or opt-out).
findings.finding: The plan's title promises 'tests + one-time capability check' but the body also introduces a third artifact — a usage-plausibility telemetry warning — without reflecting this in the title or acknowledging that runner.py's construction path will now raise for previously-passing callers.
findings.location: ## What — 'A usage-plausibility telemetry warning (never a block)'; AC — 'Usage-plausibility emits a telemetry warning and never blocks a gate'; title: 'Agent-build invariants as tests + one-time capability check'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the new cached capability check *extends* the existing `_pai_check_config` seam (preferred — single construction-time check path) or introduces a separate cached function alongside it, and state that decision explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new cached construction-time capability check in runner.py without specifying whether it extends the existing `_pai_check_config` seam or introduces a parallel cached function alongside it. The existing `_pai_check_config` already serves as the construction-time validation seam (called from both `preflight()` and `run()`), making a parallel new cached function a mild NIH-vs-extend situation.
findings.location: ## What — 'A one-time capability assertion at model construction (cached, not per-call)' / Files: src/rebar/llm/runner.py (construction-time check)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `_pai_check_config` with the tool-calling capability assertion (add an `execution_mode` parameter or check it from config); decorate with `@functools.lru_cache` on a thin wrapper if per-model caching is needed — keeping a single construction-time check seam instead of two.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific new test file(s) to be created in `tests/unit/` (e.g., `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py`) so the edit target is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan targets `tests/unit/` for new test files but names no specific test file(s), making it impossible to verify the edit target will exist or to confirm which existing test file (if any) is being extended versus which new file is being created.
findings.location: Files section: `tests/unit/` (new)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the new symbol (e.g., `_assert_provider_tool_capable(resolved_model: str)` decorated with `@lru_cache`) to be added in `runner.py` so the caching contract and the AC test target are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan promises a 'construction-time capability assertion at model construction (cached, not per-call)' in `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` but names no symbol for this new function/check, leaving the implementation location within the file ambiguous (could be in `__init__`, `preflight`, a new module-level helper, or elsewhere).
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` (construction-time check); What section: 'A one-time capability assertion at model construction (cached, not per-call)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
